name: pyqt5

metadata.name: PyQt5
metadata.summary: Python bindings for the Qt cross platform application toolkit

documentation:
# PyQt5 - Comprehensive Python Bindings for Qt v5

Qt is set of cross-platform C++ libraries that implement high-level APIs for
accessing many aspects of modern desktop and mobile systems.  These include
location and positioning services, multimedia, NFC and Bluetooth connectivity,
a Chromium based web browser, as well as traditional UI development.

PyQt5 is a comprehensive set of Python bindings for Qt v5.  It is implemented
as more than 35 extension modules and enables Python to be used as an
alternative application development language to C++ on all supported platforms
including iOS and Android.

PyQt5 may also be embedded in C++ based applications to allow users of those
applications to configure or enhance the functionality of those applications.


## Author

PyQt5 is copyright (c) Riverbank Computing Limited.  Its homepage is
https://www.riverbankcomputing.com/software/pyqt/.

Support may be obtained from the PyQt mailing list at
https://www.riverbankcomputing.com/mailman/listinfo/pyqt/.


## License

PyQt5 is released under the GPL v3 license and under a commercial license that
allows for the development of proprietary applications.


## Documentation

The documentation for the latest release can be found
[here](https://www.riverbankcomputing.com/static/Docs/PyQt5/).


## Installation

The GPL version of PyQt5 can be installed from PyPI:

    pip install PyQt5

`pip` will also build and install the bindings from the sdist package but Qt's
`qmake` tool must be on `PATH`.

The `sip-install` tool will also install the bindings from the sdist package
but will allow you to configure many aspects of the installation.

methods.name: examples.remoteobjects.simpleswitch.registryconnecteddynamicserver.SimpleSwitch.pushCurrState
documentation: PyQt does not support the use of static source types defined in .rep
files.  However we can manually specify a dynamic type that matches a
.rep defined type by defining properties, signals and slots in the same
order.  We also have to account for any internals also generated by the
.rep generator.  At the moment this only includes an extra 'push' slot
for each property (that never seems to get called).  This allows this
example to act as a server for Qt's C++ 'directconnectclient' example.
It is not necessary when using with clients that use dynamic source types
(written using either C++ or Python).

methods.name: examples.remoteobjects.simpleswitch.registryconnecteddynamicserver.SimpleSwitch.server_slot
documentation: The slot exposed to a remote client.

values in QtCore.Qt.ISODate format, but the tree views display using
QtCore.Qt.DefaultLocaleShortDate format.

methods.name: examples.mainwindows.separations.FinalWidget.mouseMoveEvent
documentation:
If the mouse moves far enough when the left mouse button is held
down, start a drag and drop operation.

methods.name: examples.mainwindows.separations.FinalWidget.mousePressEvent
documentation:
Check for left mouse button presses in order to enable drag and
drop.

methods.name: examples.mainwindows.separations.ScreenWidget.__init__
documentation:
Initializes the paint color, the mask color (cyan, magenta, or
yellow), connects the color selector and invert checkbox to functions,
and creates a two-by-two grid layout.

methods.name: examples.mainwindows.separations.ScreenWidget.createImage
documentation:
Creates a new image by separating out the cyan, magenta, or yellow
component, depending on the mask color specified in the constructor.
The amount of the component found in each pixel of the image is used
to determine how much of a user-selected ink is used for each pixel
in the new image for the label widget.

methods.name: examples.mainwindows.separations.ScreenWidget.image
documentation: Returns a reference to the modified image.

methods.name: examples.mainwindows.separations.ScreenWidget.invertImage
documentation:
Sets whether the amount of ink applied to the canvas is to be
inverted (subtracted from the maximum value) before the ink is
applied.

methods.name: examples.mainwindows.separations.ScreenWidget.setColor
documentation:
Separate the current image into cyan, magenta, and yellow
components.  Create a representation of how each component might
appear when applied to a blank white piece of paper.

methods.name: examples.mainwindows.separations.ScreenWidget.setImage
documentation:
Records the original image selected by the user, creates a color
separation, and enables the invert image checkbox.

methods.name: examples.mainwindows.separations.Viewer.__init__
documentation:
Constructor initializes a default value for the brightness, creates
the main menu entries, and constructs a central widget that contains
enough space for images to be displayed.

methods.name: examples.mainwindows.separations.Viewer.createMenus
documentation:
Creates a main menu with two entries: a File menu, to allow the image
to be selected, and a Brightness menu to allow the brightness of the
separations to be changed.
Initially, the Brightness menu items are disabled, but the first entry in
the menu is checked to reflect the default brightness.

methods.name: examples.mainwindows.separations.Viewer.createCentralWidget
documentation:
Constructs a central widget for the window consisting of a two-by-two
grid of labels, each of which will contain an image. We restrict the
size of the labels to 256 pixels, and ensure that the window cannot
be resized.

methods.name: examples.mainwindows.separations.Viewer.chooseFile
documentation:
Provides a dialog window to allow the user to specify an image file.
If a file is selected, the appropriate function is called to process
and display it.

methods.name: examples.mainwindows.separations.Viewer.setBrightness
documentation:
Changes the value of the brightness according to the entry selected in the
Brightness menu. The selected entry is checked, and the previously selected
entry is unchecked.
The color separations are updated to use the new value for the brightness.

methods.name: examples.mainwindows.separations.Viewer.openImageFile
documentation:
Load the image from the file given, and create four pixmaps based
on the original image.
The window caption is set, and the Brightness menu enabled if the image file
can be loaded.

methods.name: examples.mainwindows.separations.Viewer.createImage
documentation:
Creates an image by combining the contents of the three screens
to present a page preview.
The image associated with each screen is separated into cyan,
magenta, and yellow components. We add up the values for each
component from the three screen images, and subtract the totals
from the maximum value for each corresponding primary color.

methods.name: examples.mainwindows.separations.Viewer.saveImage
documentation: Provides a dialog window to allow the user to save the image file.

classes.name: examples.ipc.sharedmemory.sharedmemory.Dialog

methods.name: examples.ipc.sharedmemory.sharedmemory.Dialog.loadFromFile
documentation:
This slot function is called when the "Load Image From File..."
button is pressed on the firs Dialog process.  First, it tests whether
the process is already connected to a shared memory segment and, if so,
detaches from that segment.  This ensures that we always start the
example from the beginning if we run it multiple times with the same
two Dialog processes.  After detaching from an existing shared memory
segment, the user is prompted to select an image file.  The selected
file is loaded into a QImage.  The QImage is displayed in the Dialog
and streamed into a QBuffer with a QDataStream.  Next, it gets a new
shared memory segment from the system big enough to hold the image data
in the QBuffer, and it locks the segment to prevent the second Dialog
process from accessing it.  Then it copies the image from the QBuffer
into the shared memory segment.  Finally, it unlocks the shared memory
segment so the second Dialog process can access it.  After self
function runs, the user is expected to press the "Load Image from
Shared Memory" button on the second Dialog process.

methods.name: examples.ipc.sharedmemory.sharedmemory.Dialog.loadFromMemory
documentation:
This slot function is called in the second Dialog process, when the
user presses the "Load Image from Shared Memory" button.  First, it
attaches the process to the shared memory segment created by the first
Dialog process.  Then it locks the segment for exclusive access, copies
the image data from the segment into a QBuffer, and streams the QBuffer
into a QImage.  Then it unlocks the shared memory segment, detaches
from it, and finally displays the QImage in the Dialog.

methods.name: examples.ipc.sharedmemory.sharedmemory.Dialog.detach
documentation:
This private function is called by the destructor to detach the
process from its shared memory segment.  When the last process detaches
from a shared memory segment, the system releases the shared memory.

This class is a simple example of how to use QSharedMemory.  It is a
simple dialog that presents a few buttons.  Run the executable twice to
create two processes running the dialog.  In one of the processes, press
the button to load an image into a shared memory segment, and then select
an image file to load.  Once the first process has loaded and displayed the
image, in the second process, press the button to read the same image from
shared memory.  The second process displays the same image loaded from its
new location in shared memory.

The class contains a data member sharedMemory, which is initialized with
the key "QSharedMemoryExample" to force all instances of Dialog to access
the same shared memory segment.  The constructor also connects the
clicked() signal from each of the three dialog buttons to the slot function
appropriate for handling each button.

modules.name: examples.webkit.framecapture
documentation:
Capture a web page and save its internal frames in different images

  framecapture.py <url> <outputfile>

Notes:
  'url' is the URL of the web page to be captured
  'outputfile' is the prefix of the image files to be generated

Example:
  framecapture qt.nokia.com trolltech.png

Result:
  trolltech.png (full page)
  trolltech_frame1.png (...) trolltech_frameN.png ('N' number of internal frames)
!/usr/bin/env python

classes.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.pythonconsoleplugin.PythonConsolePlugin

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.pythonconsoleplugin.PythonConsolePlugin.__init__
documentation: The __init__() method is only used to set up the plugin and define its
initialized variable.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.pythonconsoleplugin.PythonConsolePlugin.initialize
documentation: The initialize() and isInitialized() methods allow the plugin to set up
any required resources, ensuring that this can only happen once for each
plugin.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.pythonconsoleplugin.PythonConsolePlugin.createWidget
documentation: This factory method creates new instances of our custom widget with the
appropriate parent.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.pythonconsoleplugin.PythonConsolePlugin.name
documentation: This method returns the name of the custom widget class that is provided
by this plugin.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.pythonconsoleplugin.PythonConsolePlugin.group
documentation: Returns the name of the group in Qt Designer's widget box that this
widget belongs to.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.pythonconsoleplugin.PythonConsolePlugin.icon
documentation: Returns the icon used to represent the custom widget in Qt Designer's
widget box.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.pythonconsoleplugin.PythonConsolePlugin.toolTip
documentation: Returns a short description of the custom widget for use in a tool tip.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.pythonconsoleplugin.PythonConsolePlugin.whatsThis
documentation: Returns a short description of the custom widget for use in a "What's
This?" help message for the widget.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.pythonconsoleplugin.PythonConsolePlugin.isContainer
documentation: Returns True if the custom widget acts as a container for other widgets;
otherwise returns False. Note that plugins for custom containers also
need to provide an implementation of the QDesignerContainerExtension
interface if they need to add custom editing support to Qt Designer.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.pythonconsoleplugin.PythonConsolePlugin.domXml
documentation: Returns an XML description of a custom widget instance that describes
default values for its properties. Each custom widget created by this
plugin will be configured using this description.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.pythonconsoleplugin.PythonConsolePlugin.includeFile
documentation: Returns the module containing the custom widget class. It may include
a module path.

PythonConsolePlugin(QPyDesignerCustomWidgetPlugin)

Provides a Python custom plugin for Qt Designer by implementing the
QDesignerCustomWidgetPlugin via a PyQt-specific custom plugin class.

documentation: Return True if the custom widget has been intialised.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.pydemoplugin.PyDemoPlugin.createWidget
documentation: Return a new instance of the custom widget with the given parent.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.pydemoplugin.PyDemoPlugin.name
documentation: Return the name of the class that implements the custom widget.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.pydemoplugin.PyDemoPlugin.group
documentation: Return the name of the group to which the custom widget belongs.  A new
group will be created if it doesn't already exist.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.pydemoplugin.PyDemoPlugin.icon
documentation: Return the icon used to represent the custom widget in Designer's widget
box.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.pydemoplugin.PyDemoPlugin.toolTip
documentation: Return a short description of the custom widget used by Designer in a
tool tip.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.pydemoplugin.PyDemoPlugin.whatsThis
documentation: Return a full description of the custom widget used by Designer in
"What's This?" help for the widget.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.pydemoplugin.PyDemoPlugin.isContainer
documentation: Return True if the custom widget acts as a container for other widgets.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.pydemoplugin.PyDemoPlugin.domXml
documentation: Return an XML fragment that allows the default values of the custom
widget's properties to be overridden.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.pydemoplugin.PyDemoPlugin.includeFile
documentation: Return the name of the module containing the class that implements the
custom widget.  It may include a module path.
This class implements the interface expected by Qt Designer to access the
custom widget.  See the description of the QDesignerCustomWidgetInterface
class for full details.

classes.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.polygonwidgetplugin.PolygonWidgetPlugin

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.polygonwidgetplugin.PolygonWidgetPlugin.__init__
documentation: The __init__() method is only used to set up the plugin and define its
initialized variable.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.polygonwidgetplugin.PolygonWidgetPlugin.initialize
documentation: The initialize() and isInitialized() methods allow the plugin to set up
any required resources, ensuring that this can only happen once for each
plugin.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.polygonwidgetplugin.PolygonWidgetPlugin.createWidget
documentation: This factory method creates new instances of our custom widget with the
appropriate parent.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.polygonwidgetplugin.PolygonWidgetPlugin.name
documentation: This method returns the name of the custom widget class that is provided
by this plugin.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.polygonwidgetplugin.PolygonWidgetPlugin.group
documentation: Returns the name of the group in Qt Designer's widget box that this
widget belongs to.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.polygonwidgetplugin.PolygonWidgetPlugin.icon
documentation: Returns the icon used to represent the custom widget in Qt Designer's
widget box.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.polygonwidgetplugin.PolygonWidgetPlugin.toolTip
documentation: Returns a short description of the custom widget for use in a tool tip.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.polygonwidgetplugin.PolygonWidgetPlugin.whatsThis
documentation: Returns a short description of the custom widget for use in a "What's
This?" help message for the widget.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.polygonwidgetplugin.PolygonWidgetPlugin.isContainer
documentation: Returns True if the custom widget acts as a container for other widgets;
otherwise returns False. Note that plugins for custom containers also
need to provide an implementation of the QDesignerContainerExtension
interface if they need to add custom editing support to Qt Designer.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.polygonwidgetplugin.PolygonWidgetPlugin.domXml
documentation: Returns an XML description of a custom widget instance that describes
default values for its properties. Each custom widget created by this
plugin will be configured using this description.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.python.polygonwidgetplugin.PolygonWidgetPlugin.includeFile
documentation: Returns the module containing the custom widget class. It may include
a module path.

PolygonWidgetPlugin(QPyDesignerCustomWidgetPlugin)

Provides a Python custom plugin for Qt Designer by implementing the
QDesignerCustomWidgetPlugin via a PyQt-specific custom plugin class.

enable the value to be incremented and decremented.

Provides a custom label widget to be used as a counter, with signals
similar to those provided by QAbstractSlider subclasses and properties
similar to those provided by QLabel.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.widgets.analogclock.PyAnalogClock.getTimeZone
documentation: The timeZone property is implemented using the getTimeZone() getter
method, the setTimeZone() setter method, and the resetTimeZone() method.
The getter just returns the internal time zone value.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.widgets.analogclock.PyAnalogClock.setTimeZone
documentation: The setTimeZone() method is also defined to be a slot. The @pyqtSlot
decorator is used to tell PyQt which argument type the method expects,
and is especially useful when you want to define slots with the same
name that accept different argument types.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.widgets.analogclock.PyAnalogClock.resetTimeZone
documentation: Qt's property system supports properties that can be reset to their
original values. This method enables the timeZone property to be reset.

PyAnalogClock(QWidget)

Provides an analog clock custom widget with signals, slots and properties.
The implementation is based on the Analog Clock example provided with both
Qt and PyQt.

HelloGLWidget(QOpenGLWidget)

Provides a custom widget to display an OpenGL-rendered Qt logo.
Various properties and slots are defined so that the user can rotate
the logo, and signals are defined to enable other components to
react to changes to its orientation.

If pluginType is MODULE, the plugin loader will call moduleInformation.  The
variable MODULE is inserted into the local namespace by the plugin loader.

functions.name: pyuic.uic.widget-plugins.qtprintsupport.moduleInformation
documentation: moduleInformation() must return a tuple (module, widget_list).  If "module"
is "A" and any widget from this module is used, the code generator will write
"import A".  If "module" is "A[.B].C", the code generator will write
"from A[.B] import C".  Each entry in "widget_list" must be unique.

functions.name: pyuic.uic.widget-plugins.qtcharts.moduleInformation
documentation: moduleInformation() must return a tuple (module, widget_list).  If "module"
is "A" and any widget from this module is used, the code generator will write
"import A".  If "module" is "A[.B].C", the code generator will write
"from A[.B] import C".  Each entry in "widget_list" must be unique.

classes.name: examples.designer.plugins.widgets.bubbleswidget.BaseClass

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.widgets.bubbleswidget.BaseClass.getAuthor
documentation: Define getter, setter and resetter methods for the author property.

BaseClass(QWidget)

Provides a base custom widget class to show that properties implemented
in Python can be inherited and shown as belonging to distinct classes
in Qt Designer's Property Editor.

classes.name: examples.designer.plugins.widgets.bubbleswidget.Bubble
documentation:
Bubble

Provides a class to represent individual bubbles in a BubblesWidget.
Each Bubble instance can render itself onto a paint device using a
QPainter passed to its drawBubble() method.

classes.name: examples.designer.plugins.widgets.bubbleswidget.BubblesWidget

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.widgets.bubbleswidget.BubblesWidget.getBubbles
documentation: We provide getter and setter methods for the numberOfBubbles property.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.widgets.bubbleswidget.BubblesWidget.setBubbles
documentation: The setBubbles() method can also be used as a slot.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.widgets.bubbleswidget.BubblesWidget.getColor1
documentation: We provide getter and setter methods for the color1 and color2
properties. The red, green and blue components for the QColor
values stored in these properties can be edited individually in
Qt Designer.

methods.name: examples.designer.plugins.widgets.bubbleswidget.BubblesWidget.stop
documentation: The stop() and start() slots provide simple control over the animation
of the bubbles in the widget.

BubblesWidget(BaseClass)

Provides a custom widget that shows a number of rising bubbles.
Various properties are defined so that the user can customize the
appearance of the widget, and change the number and behaviour of the
bubbles shown.

functions.name: pyuic.uic.compileUiDir
documentation:
compileUiDir(dir, recurse=False, map=None, **compileUi_args)

Creates Python modules from Qt Designer .ui files in a directory or
directory tree.

dir is the name of the directory to scan for files whose name ends with
'.ui'.  By default the generated Python module is created in the same
directory ending with '.py'.
recurse is set if any sub-directories should be scanned.  The default is
False.
map is an optional callable that is passed the name of the directory
containing the '.ui' file and the name of the Python module that will be
created.  The callable should return a tuple of the name of the directory
in which the Python module will be created and the (possibly modified)
name of the module.  The default is None.
compileUi_args are any additional keyword arguments that are passed to
the compileUi() function that is called to create each Python module.

functions.name: pyuic.uic.compileUi
documentation:
compileUi(uifile, pyfile, execute=False, indent=4, from_imports=False, resource_suffix='\_rc', import_from='.')

Creates a Python module from a Qt Designer .ui file.

uifile is a file name or file-like object containing the .ui file.
pyfile is the file-like object to which the Python code will be written to.
execute is optionally set to generate extra Python code that allows the
code to be run as a standalone application.  The default is False.
indent is the optional indentation width using spaces.  If it is 0 then a
tab is used.  The default is 4.
from_imports is optionally set to generate relative import statements.  At
the moment this only applies to the import of resource modules.
resource_suffix is the suffix appended to the basename of any resource file
specified in the .ui file to create the name of the Python module generated
from the resource file by pyrcc4.  The default is '\_rc', i.e. if the .ui
file specified a resource file called foo.qrc then the corresponding Python
module is foo_rc.
import_from is optionally set to the package used for relative import
statements.  The default is `'.'`.

functions.name: pyuic.uic.loadUiType
documentation:
loadUiType(uifile, from_imports=False, resource_suffix='\_rc', import_from='.') -> (form class, base class)

Load a Qt Designer .ui file and return the generated form class and the Qt
base class.

uifile is a file name or file-like object containing the .ui file.
from_imports is optionally set to generate relative import statements.  At
the moment this only applies to the import of resource modules.
resource_suffix is the suffix appended to the basename of any resource file
specified in the .ui file to create the name of the Python module generated
from the resource file by pyrcc4.  The default is '\_rc', i.e. if the .ui
file specified a resource file called foo.qrc then the corresponding Python
module is foo_rc.
import_from is optionally set to the package used for relative import
statements.  The default is `'.'`.

functions.name: pyuic.uic.loadUi
documentation:
loadUi(uifile, baseinstance=None, package='') -> widget

Load a Qt Designer .ui file and return an instance of the user interface.

uifile is a file name or file-like object containing the .ui file.
baseinstance is an optional instance of the Qt base class.  If specified
then the user interface is created in it.  Otherwise a new instance of the
base class is automatically created.
package is the optional package which is used as the base for any relative
imports of custom widgets.
resource_suffix is the suffix appended to the basename of any resource file
specified in the .ui file to create the name of the Python module generated
from the resource file by pyrcc4.  The default is '_rc', i.e. if the .ui
file specified a resource file called foo.qrc then the corresponding Python
module is foo_rc.

Create a unique name from a string.
\>>> p = UIParser(QtCore, QtGui, QtWidgets)
\>>> p.uniqueName("foo")

\>>> p.uniqueName("foo")

classes.name: pyuic.uic.Compiler.qtproxies.ProxyClassMember

methods.name: pyuic.uic.Compiler.qtproxies.ProxyClassMember.__getattribute__
documentation:
Reimplemented to create a proxy connect() if requested and this
might be a proxy for a signal.

methods.name: pyuic.uic.Compiler.qtproxies.ProxyClassMember.__getitem__
documentation:
Reimplemented to create a proxy member that should be a signal that
passes arguments.  We handle signals without arguments before we get
here and never apply the index notation to them.
ATTENTION: currently, classes can either be literal or normal. If a class
should need both kinds of behaviour, the code has to be changed.

classes.name: pyuic.uic.Compiler.qtproxies.ProxySignalWithArguments
documentation: This is a proxy for (what should be) a signal that passes arguments.

classes.name: pyuic.uic.Compiler.qtproxies.LiteralProxyClass
documentation:
LiteralObject(args) -> new literal class

a literal class can be used as argument in a function call

```pycon
>>> class Foo(LiteralProxyClass): pass
>>> str(Foo(1,2,3)) == "Foo(1,2,3)"
```

classes.name: pyuic.uic.Compiler.qtproxies.QtCore

inner_classes.name: pyuic.uic.Compiler.qtproxies.QtCore.QMetaObject
documentation: # connectSlotsByName and connect have to be handled as class methods,
# otherwise they would be created as LiteralProxyClasses and never be
# printed
These are all the Qt classes used by pyuic5 in their namespaces. If a class
is missing, the compiler will fail, normally with an AttributeError.
For adding new classes:
- utility classes used as literal values do not need to be listed
because they are created on the fly as subclasses of LiteralProxyClass
- classes which are *not* QWidgets inherit from ProxyClass and they
have to be listed explicitly in the correct namespace. These classes
are created via a ProxyQObjectCreator
- new QWidget-derived classes have to inherit from qtproxies.QWidget
If the widget does not need any special methods, it can be listed
in _qwidgets

inner_classes.name: pyuic.uic.Compiler.qtproxies.QtWidgets.QAction
documentation: QActions inherit from QObject for the meta-object stuff and the hierarchy
has to be correct since we have a isinstance(x, QtWidgets.QLayout) call
in the UI parser.
